title: Quiz: Quantum Kernels
description: Test your understanding of quantum kernel methods and feature maps

questions.question: What does a quantum kernel k_Q(x, x') compute?

options.text: The Euclidean distance between data points
The squared overlap |<phi(x')|phi(x)>|^2 of quantum feature states
The expectation value of the Hamiltonian
The entropy of the quantum state

questions.explanation: The quantum kernel computes k_Q(x, x') = |<phi(x')|phi(x)>|^2, which is the squared magnitude of the inner product between quantum feature states. This measures the similarity between data points in the quantum feature space.

questions.question: In the ZZ feature map, what is the purpose of the ZZ entangling rotations?

options.text: To reduce the circuit depth
To encode correlations between different features of the input
To measure the quantum state
To implement error correction

questions.explanation: ZZ rotations with angles depending on x_i * x_j encode correlations (interactions) between different input features. This creates entanglement in the feature state that captures non-linear relationships in the data.

questions.question: How is the quantum kernel estimated on real quantum hardware?

options.text: By directly computing the inner product of state vectors
By applying U(x), then U^dag(x'), and measuring probability of |0...0>
By performing quantum tomography
By using amplitude estimation

questions.explanation: On hardware, we prepare |0>, apply U(x), then U^dag(x'), and measure. The probability of getting |0...0> equals |<0|U^dag(x')U(x)|0>|^2 = |<phi(x')|phi(x)>|^2, which is the kernel value.

questions.question: What is kernel-target alignment used to measure?

options.text: The number of support vectors
How well the kernel separates different classes
The computational complexity of the kernel
The noise level in the quantum circuit

questions.explanation: Kernel-target alignment measures the correlation between the kernel matrix K and the ideal kernel matrix Y_outer = y*y^T. High alignment indicates the kernel produces large values for same-class pairs and small values for different-class pairs.

questions.question: Quantum kernels always outperform classical kernels for machine learning tasks.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. Quantum advantage depends on the data structure matching the quantum feature map. For many datasets, classical kernels (RBF, polynomial) perform equally well or better. Quantum kernels excel when data has structure that quantum circuits naturally capture.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of projected quantum kernels over standard quantum kernels?

options.text: They require more qubits
They have O(n) scaling instead of O(n^2) for kernel matrix computation
They always achieve higher accuracy
They don't require quantum hardware

questions.explanation: Projected kernels extract classical feature vectors by measuring expectation values of observables. The kernel matrix can then be computed as F*F^T with O(n) quantum circuit evaluations, compared to O(n^2) for standard quantum kernels.

questions.question: What determines the expressiveness of a quantum kernel?

options.text: Only the number of qubits
Only the circuit depth
The choice of feature map and how well it captures data structure
The classical optimizer used for SVM training

questions.explanation: Kernel expressiveness depends on the feature map design - the gates used, their connectivity, and how data is encoded. A good feature map produces distinguishable states for different data points while grouping similar data.

questions.question: Which of the following are true about quantum feature maps? (Select all that apply)

options.text: They map classical data to quantum states
The feature space dimension is 2^n for n qubits
They must be implemented with only single-qubit gates
Repeated layers (reps) can increase expressiveness

questions.explanation: Feature maps encode classical data x as quantum states |phi(x)> = U(x)|0>. The Hilbert space has dimension 2^n. Multi-qubit entangling gates (not just single-qubit) are typically used. Increasing repetitions (layers) generally increases expressiveness.
